ECO10005-Economics for Business
   Decision Making

   Monetary Policy



    Professor Abbas Valadkhani
    Week 10












1









                OBJECIVE, FRAMEWORK AND TOOLS
  Monetary Policy Objectives
       –The objective of monetary policy is set out in the
          Reserve Bank Act 1959           .
   –(a) the stability of the currency of Australia;
   –(b) the maintenance of full employment in Australia;
   –(c) the economic prosperity and welfare of the
      Australians.
   –(d) Keeping inflation between 2 and 3 per cent, on
      average, over the business cycle.
       •A monetary policy strategy in which the central bank
          commits to an explicit inflation target and to
          explaining how its actions will achieve that target is
          called  inflation targeting     .

2

          The slope of the demand curve
                       (move along curve)

       The demand curve for bank reserves is
        downward sloping to show the inverse
        relationship between the cash rate quantity of
        reserves demanded by banks.
       The cash rate is the opportunity cost to banks of
        holding reserves on deposit at the Reserve Bank.
            –   Low interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding
                money.
            –   High interest rates increase the opportunity cost of
                holding money.

               OBJECIVE, FRAMEWORK AND TOOLS
     1.When the Reserve
         Bank wants to slow
         inflation, it raises the
         cash rate target.

     2.When the inflation
         rate is below target
         and the Reserve
         Bank wants to avoid
         recession, it lowers
         the cash rate.

     3.And keeps lowering it.

           1.The first effect of a
                 monetary policy decision
                 by the Reserve Bank
                 board is a change in the
                 cash rate.
           2.Other short term interest
                 rates then change
                 quickly and predictably.

     Does the cash rate typically flows
            through the financial system

   •The financial system is composed of may markets, such as
        overnight interbank loans, 90-day bank bills, 180-day bank bills,
        and home mortgages (each of which are an asset).
   •Each of these assets has its own market. In the text these
        individual markets are combined into a single market called the
        “market for loanable funds”. Demand and supply forces interact
        in this market to determine the interest rate.
   •The loanable funds model is concerned with the long-term real
        rate of interest.
   •In the money market model, the focus is on the short-term
        nominal rate of interest.

                                                                                               18
18




                                                                                                                                        9

      When conducting monetary policy, it is the short-
       term nominal interest rate that is most affected by
       increases and decreases in liquidity.
      These markets are usually closely linked, so if
       “cash” rate goes up, interest rates in the other
       markets tend to rise too.  Similar results apply
       with a fall in the “cash” interest rate.
            − These assets are close substitutes but it appears
              that the relationship is weakening.








   19

19















































                                   HBE 110 Week 3 20

20




                                                                                                          10

21




              The demand for money

     The money demand curve is downward sloping to
      show the inverse relationship between the
      interest rate on financial assets and the quantity
      of money demanded.
     The interest rate on financial assets is the
      opportunity cost of holding money:
          –  Low interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding
             money.
          –  High interest rates increase the opportunity cost of
             holding money.




22




                                                                                                   11

      The demand for money: Figure 17.2






















23




      Shifts in the money demand curve:
                      Figure 17.3




















24




                                                                             12

         How the RBA manages the
                     supply of cash
     Repurchase agreements are widely used by the
      RBA in its OMOs.

     Cash rate:    The interest rate that financial
      institutions charge on loans or pay to borrow funds
      in the overnight money market.

     The cash rate is determined by the interaction of
      demand for and supply of funds in the overnight
      money market.




25




         How the RBA manages the
                     supply of cash
     Exchange settlement accounts (ESAs):
      Accounts held with the RBA by banks and other
      financial institutions to enable the overnight
      transfer of funds (cash) between financial
      institutions, and between the RBA and financial
      institutions.
     ESA’s enable real-time gross settlement (RTGS)
      through the RBA information and transfer system
      (RITS).
     Balances held in ESAs are called          exchange
      settlement funds.

26




                                                                                           13

     The market for loanable funds: Figure
                               17.7




















27




             Can the RBA eliminate
       contractions and recessions?

    Keeping a contraction or recession milder than it
     would otherwise have been is usually the best the
     RBA can do.
    There may be a      recognition lag    –a delay before the
     RBA recognises that a contraction is imminent.
    The longest time lag with monetary policy is the
     impact lag    –the time taken for monetary policy to
     affect real GDP.


    28


28




                                                                                         14

       Using monetary policy to fight
                             inflation

     Sometimes, aggregate demand may be increasing
      too fast, leading to inflation, and the economy may
      be in equilibrium beyond potential GDP.


     Contractionary monetary policy          (increase interest
      rates to reduce inflation) is used during periods of
      high or rising inflation rates.
